# Page Structures

Choose a page structure before drafting. The structure should match the reader's
job and the surrounding site.

## Landing Or Index Page

Use for section entry points and product-level orientation.

- Front matter when the site uses page titles, descriptions, icons, or hidden
  navigation controls.
- One H1 matching the product, section, or capability.
- A short lead that states what the reader can do here.
- Route cards or grouped links for the main paths.
- One concrete starter example or workflow when useful.
- A final "next paths" section only when it helps navigation.

## Tutorial Or How-To

Use when the reader needs to complete a task.

- State prerequisites and expected outcome.
- Use numbered steps for sequential work.
- Include commands from the repo root.
- Show verification immediately after the action it proves.
- Put alternatives in tabs or separate subsections.

## Reference Page

Use when the reader needs lookup accuracy.

- Define the contract the page documents.
- Group entries by stable concepts, not implementation accident.
- Use tables only for compact lookup data.
- Include schema, command, generated-output, or source ownership when relevant.
- State how the reference is regenerated or checked.

## Concept Or Design Page

Use when the reader needs the model behind the system.

- Start with the problem and decision boundary.
- Name the core concepts before detailing implementation.
- Use diagrams for state, dependency, or request flow.
- Separate current behavior from proposed behavior.
- Preserve tradeoffs and rejected alternatives when they affect future choices.

## Process Page

Use when the reader needs to repeat a workflow.

- Define entry conditions and exit criteria.
- Identify owners, tools, and validation commands.
- Keep durable workflow steps separate from one-time migration notes.
- Link to generated state, manifests, or logs instead of copying long outputs.
